coredata.dc:title: Near-UV-emitting graphene quantum dots from graphene hydrogels
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Hongyi
Gong, Tao
Jin, Yinhua
Cho, Yujin
Shin, Cheolmin
Lee, Changgu
Kim, Taesung
coredata.dc:description: We report a novel method to prepare graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from graphene hydrogels. Graphene hydrogels were prepared using a hydrothermal technique, and GQDs were released from the hydrogels on immersion of the hydrogels in low-polarity organic solvents. This method did not require additional treatments such as the centrifugation, filtration and dialysis typical of the general hydrothermal method. These GQDs were observed to fluoresce, with their strongest emission in the near-UV region, at ∼347nm. Moreover, these GQDs, when in their pure state, formed a highly viscous liquid insoluble in water due to their lack of many oxygen-containing functional groups.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the reduced graphene oxide (rGO) hydrogel

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Global Frontier R&D Program on Center for Multiscale Energy System
Ministry of Education, Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank Jingling Liu from SKKU SAINT for her kind help. This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea ( NRF ) funded by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technology ( NRF-2013R1A1A2058893 ) and the Global Frontier R&D Program on Center for Multiscale Energy System ( NRF-2012M3A6A7054863 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis procedure of GQDs. (a) Steps involved in the preparation of near-UV-emitting GQDs, with associated photographs and schematic diagrams. The photograph of the photoluminescent rGO hydrogel in hexane was obtained under excitation at 365nm. (b) Model of a portion of the hydrogel in water, with GQDs adsorbed on the surface of large rGO sheets using π–π stacking interactions. (c) Model of a portion of the hydrogel in an organic solvent, with the photoluminescent GQDs exfoliated from the surface of the rGO sheet by the organic solvent. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

HRTEM and AFM images of the GQDs. (a) HRTEM image of these GQDs. (b) Fringe patterns of GQDs by HRTEM. (c) AFM image of the GQDs. (d) The height profile along the line labeled “1” in the image in panel (c). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) FT-IR spectra of hexane, GQDs, pyrene, benzo[e]pyrene, rGO hydrogel and GO. Since the GQDs, pyrene, benzo[e]pyrene and the rGO hydrogel were dissolved in hexane, the FT-IR spectrum of hexane was used as reference. Dried GO powder was used for FT-IR measurements. (b) From left to right, GQDs in acetone (under visible light and 365nm UV light), and GQDs in a mixture of acetone and water (under visible light and 365nm UV light). (c) MALDI-TOF mass spectrum of GQDs using 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid (DHB) as the matrix; the inset image is a hypothetical structure of a GQD based on 158 carbon atoms. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

GQD in different organic solvents and GQD composites (a, b) Photographs of GQDs dispersed in different organic solvents: (from left to right) THF, acetone, ethanol, DCM, ether, chloroform, toluene and hexane. Photographs were taken under (a) visible light and (b) under UV light (365nm). (c) Photograph of the transparent PDMS+GQDs composite under visible light, which was prepared by curing the mixture of Dow Corning SYLGARD® 184 and the GQD/THF solution in an oven at 80°C for 4 hours. The mass ratio of GQDs in the PDMS is ∼4.59ppm. (d) Photograph showing the blue photoluminescence of the PDMS+GQD composite under UV light (365nm). (e, f) Photographs of dry GQDs on an etched quartz glass plate taken under (e) visible light and (f) UV light (365nm). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Photoluminescence spectra of GQDs in hexane at different excitation wavelengths. (b) UV–visible absorption spectrum of GQDs in hexane (black curve labeled “Abs”), photoluminescence excitation spectrum of GQDs in hexane with the detection wavelength of 347nm (blue curve labeled “Ex”), and photoluminescence emission spectra of GQDs in different organic solvents under 295nm excitation (various colored curves above the label “Em”). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Elemental analysis of the GO, rGO hydrogel and GQDs produced by the present method. C (%), H (%) and O (%) are based on the weight percentage of the element. NC/O is the atomic ratio of C to O.

head.ce:title: Near-UV-emitting graphene quantum dots from graphene hydrogels

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: SKKU Advanced Institute of Nano Technology (SAINT), Sungkyunkwan University, Suwon 440-746, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SKKU Advanced Institute of Nano Technology (SAINT)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, Sungkyunkwan University, Suwon 440-746, South Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Mechanical Engineering, Sungkyunkwan University, Suwon 440-746, South Korea. Fax: +82 31 290 7930 (C. Lee), +82 31 290 5889 (T. Kim).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a novel method to prepare graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from graphene hydrogels. Graphene hydrogels were prepared using a hydrothermal technique, and GQDs were released from the hydrogels on immersion of the hydrogels in low-polarity organic solvents. This method did not require additional treatments such as the centrifugation, filtration and dialysis typical of the general hydrothermal method. These GQDs were observed to fluoresce, with their strongest emission in the near-UV region, at ∼347nm. Moreover, these GQDs, when in their pure state, formed a highly viscous liquid insoluble in water due to their lack of many oxygen-containing functional groups.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as one of the derivatives of graphene, have attracted much attention because of their unique spin, electronic and optical properties [1]. Graphene is a zero-bandgap semimetal that displays bandgap closing at Dirac points [2], and its size can be controlled to be from several micrometers to 30inches [3]. In principle, the bandgap of graphene can be tuned from 0eV to that of benzene by varying its size [4,5]. GQDs, having dimensions of less than 20nm, can be considered as ultra-small fragments of graphene and at the same time as extended polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [6]. The bandgap of small GQDs widens due to the quantum confinement effect. GQDs, finite-sized semi-metallic graphene, thus become semiconductors, which greatly extends the applications of graphene materials to fields such as optoelectronic devices, sensors and bio-imaging [5].

Various methods of synthesizing GQDs have been reported, such as hydrothermal synthesis [7], solvothermal [8] and electrochemical methods [9], microwave technology [10] and the bottom-up method [11,12]. The hydrothermal approach is a relatively simple method. However, after hydrothermal cutting of graphene sheets, the resulting colloidal solution still generally contains some large graphene nanoparticles that quench the photoluminescence of the GQDs [13–16]. Therefore, it has been necessary after such hydrothermal cutting to purify the GQDs, a process that takes a long time to carry out [7].

Herein, we report a novel, facile and “slow-release” method to prepare near-UV-emitting GQDs from graphene hydrogels. This hydrothermal method does not require follow-up treatments such as centrifugation, filtration and dialysis. The graphene hydrogel, which is another form and derivative of graphene, has been studied for several years. Generally, the three-dimensional (3D) porous graphene hydrogel is synthesized by a hydrothermal approach based on self-assembly and π–π stacking interactions of graphene sheets in water [17]. In our study, the 3D porous graphene hydrogel was designed to be a source of GQDs by immersing the hydrogel in a low-polarity organic solvent, thereby releasing GQDs into the solvent. These GQDs were observed to display a strongest fluorescence emission peak at 347nm (3.57eV) in the near-UV region.

All chemicals were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich. GO was synthesized from natural graphite flakes by the modified Hummer method. 3g of graphite powder (Alfa Aesar, 325 mesh, 99.8%), 2.5g of NaNO3 and 185g H2SO4 (purity 95%) were placed in a beaker with a magnetic stirrer. The mixture was cooled for 1h using an ice water bath, and then 12g of KMnO4 was slowly added into this mixture. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 4days. 300ml of a 5wt% H2SO4 aqueous solution was added to the above solution, which was then stirred for another hour. Then, 30wt% H2O2 was slowly added until the GO solution became yellow, followed by stirring the mixture for an additional hour. Then the products were washed 20 times by centrifugation and DI water to obtain an aqueous GO solution, which was then diluted to a concentration of 2.86mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the reduced graphene oxide (rGO) hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The aqueous GO solution was subjected to a probe sonication (15kHz) for 2–4h. A 2-ml vial containing 1ml of this aqueous GO solution was then put into a Teflon-lined autoclave, heated at 180°C for 12h, and then cooled to room temperature, which yielded the rGO hydrogel in water. The size of the rGO hydrogel depended on the volume of the aqueous GO solution placed in the autoclave.

The rGO hydrogel was transferred, using tweezers, from the water to one of various tested organic solvents including tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetone, ethanol, dichloromethane (DCM), ether, chloroform, toluene and hexane. After several hours, the GQDs were observed to be completely released into the organic solvent from the rGO hydrogel. In contrast, it took two to three days for the GQDs to be completely released from a dried rGO hydrogel. The near-UV emission of the GQDs in solution was obtained after removing the rGO hydrogel using tweezers.

Oxidation of graphite by a strong oxidant is generally known to yield small sheets of exfoliated GO with dimensions of several micrometers. Many GO fragments have been observed in GO solutions with a sufficiently long-duration oxidation, such as 4days in this work. Moreover, long-duration high-intensity sonication can further cut the GO sheets into smaller pieces, with dimensions from several nanometers to hundreds of nanometers, as shown for this work in the atomic force microscopy (AFM) image of Fig. S1.

The rGO hydrogel was synthesized from the aqueous GO solution following the hydrothermal procedure in the previous report [17]. The ultrafine GO fragments were reduced to ultrafine rGO pieces after the hydrothermal treatment. Moreover, because of linear defects due to epoxy group chains on the surface of the GO sheets, the large GO sheets were also easily cut into ultrafine rGO pieces during the hydrothermal deoxidization process [7]. However, polar functional groups of GO were removed by hydrothermal reduction, rendering water, which is polar, a poor solvent for the large rGO sheets and GQDs. The formation of the rGO hydrogel from the large rGO sheets was driven by π–π stacking interactions, as depicted in Fig. 1
[17]. Simultaneously, the GQDs were also adsorbed on the surface of the large rGO sheets by π–π stacking interactions. The GQDs on the rGO hydrogel did not show any fluorescence, likely due to the ability of a large rGO sheet to quench such fluorescence using resonance energy transfer [13–16]. Note that the attractive power of π–π stacking interactions between large rGO sheets is much stronger than that between GQDs and large rGO sheets, because of the ultrafine size and ultra-small contact area of GQDs. Immersion of the rGO hydrogel into a low-polarity organic solvent apparently resulted in the van der Waals interactions between GQDs and the organic solvents strong enough to overcome the attraction of π–π stacking interactions between the GQDs and large rGO sheets. Hence, GQDs on the surface of large rGO sheets could escape from the rGO hydrogel. However, due to the large contact area and strong π–π stacking interactions, the large rGO sheets could not be separated from the rGO hydrogel. Without the influence of the large rGO sheets, the GQDs were indeed observed to display a strong fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 1.

As shown in Fig. S2, the rGO hydrogel was picked up from water and immersed into low-polarity organic sol-vents, such as tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetone, ethanol, dichloromethane (DCM), ether, chloroform, toluene and hexane. After a few seconds, the GQDs started to diffuse into the organic solvent. The GQDs were completely released from the rGO hydrogel after several hours, with the rGO hydrogel functioning like a sponge. This process is similar to the processes involved in the dissolution of a solute and the slow release of a drug [18]. The large residual rGO sheets that did not display fluorescence took the form of a bulk hydrogel, which was quite easy to remove from the GQD solution using tweezers.

The dimensions of the GQDs were determined to be from ∼2.3nm to ∼3.8nm from a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) image (Fig. 2 a). The measured ∼0.22nm in-plane distance (labeled in Fig. 2b) agrees with the lattice fringes of the [001] planes [19]. The topographic heights were measured in the AFM images (Fig. 2c–d) to be between 0.5nm and 1.5nm, which correspond to the expected thicknesses of monolayer and bilayer GQDs, respectively [10,20].

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra are shown in Fig. 3 a. Since the GQD sample was pre-pared by immersing the rGO hydrogel in a hexane solvent, the FT-IR spectrum of hexane was also measured as a reference. For the GQD sample, the new peaks in the range of 1289–832cm−1 are due to the aromatic C–H in-plane bending. The 725cm−1 peak corresponds to aromatic C–H out-of-plane bending. The 3184cm−1 peak resulted from the C–H stretch in the aromatic ring. In contrast to the FT-IR spectra of GO and of the rGO hydrogel, no obvious –OH and CO peaks were observed in the spectrum of the GQDs. This result is very different from that for other reported methods, in which the FT-IR spectra of GQDs usually have obvious and clear –OH and CO peaks [1,7,8,21,22]. The spectrum of the GQDs that we made is similar to that of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [23–26]. Two kinds of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, pyrene and benzo[e]pyrene, were also measured as references. The GQD, pyrene and benzo[e]pyrene spectra were observed to be extremely similar. Moreover, just as polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons are not soluble in water, it is also difficult to dissolve our GQDs in water. As shown in Fig. 3b, after some water was added into the GQD-acetone solution, the transparent solution became cloudy, and the fluorescence persisted, indicating the similarity of the properties of our GQDs and those of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons.

A 13C-NMR spectrum was acquired to further study the chemical structure of the GQDs, as shown in Fig. S3. In addition to the two strong solvent peaks at 67ppm and 26ppm, two obvious broad peaks, one between 108ppm and 101ppm and the other between 41ppm and 24ppm, were observed and ascribed to sp2 carbon (sp2 C) and alkyl carbon (C–H) in the GQD molecules. In addition, a carbonyl carbon (CO) peak at 201ppm and extremely weak peaks at 174ppm and 72ppm corresponding to the hydrophilic groups COOH and C–OH also appeared. An elemental analysis (EA) was also carried out to determine the content of oxygen in the GQDs (Table 1 and Fig. S4). In the GQDs, the atomic ratio of C to O (NC/O) was determined to be 19.79, which is almost 4 times larger than that determined for the rGO hydrogel (NC/O =5.30). It is also much larger than that of water-soluble GQDs in other reports [27]. To the best of our knowledge, the C/O atomic ratio of the GQDs produced by the present method is greater than that reported for any other GQDs. The obvious increase in H content of GQDs could be due to the over-oxidation of ultrafine GO fragments. It is believed that the edge carbon atoms have stronger chemical activity than that of basal plane carbon atoms. Comparing with the large GO sheets, the ultrafine GO fragments have relatively low chemical steric effect. The long-duration (4days) oxidation also leads to over-oxidation of carbon atoms in the basal plane of ultrafine GO fragments. After hydrothermal treatment, all of the defects containing oxygen in the edge and basal plane were thermally reduced to be H atom. Therefore, the GQDs have higher H atomic content. These 13C-NMR and EA results taken together indicate that the present method produced GQDs with few hydrophilic –OH and –COOH groups, and this low relative amount of hydrophilic groups result in the GQDs being poorly soluble in water.

Theoretically, the average size of GQDs can be estimated by the molecular weight and the number of carbon atoms. The molecular weight of the current GQDs was measured by analyzing MALDI-TOF mass spectra (Fig. 3(c)), in the m/z range of 1600–2300. Accordingly, the number of carbon atoms is approximately 133–192. As shown in the inset image of Fig. 3(c), a peak in the GQD mass spectrum appearing, for example, at an m/z value of 1896 is indicative of a GQD consisting of ∼158 carbon atoms and having dimensions of about 2.23nm×1.99nm as estimated based on the structural parameters of graphene (bond length: 1.42Å, bond angle: 120°). Similarly, the dimensions of a GQD consisting of 192 carbon atoms would be about 2.41nm×2.46nm (Fig. S5). These estimated sizes agree well with the above TEM measurement (average size ∼2.5nm). According to the estimated size and molecular weight of GQDs, the interaction energy of GQDs–rGO and GQDs–organic solvents can be roughly calculated in this slow-release process of GQDs (Supporting information, Part 2, Fig. S9 and Table S3). For the 158-carbon-atom GQDs molecule, the Van der Waals interaction energy of GQDs–organic solvents is ranging from ∼7.48×10−19 (J) to ∼12.45×10−19 (J). The interaction energy of GQDs–rGO is ∼7.85×10−19 (J). However, the interaction energy between GQDs and water is only ∼3.29×10−19 (J). Moreover, considering the structure defects, hydrogen and oxygen atoms in GQDs, the Van der Waals interaction between organic solvents and GQDs has enough energy to overcome the attraction between GQDs and rGO sheets, but the water cannot. Therefore, the GQDs were released from rGO hydrogel in low polar organic solvents. The MALDI-TOF data and calculation of energy strongly support our above theoretical assumption.

Various low-polarity organic solvents, such as THF, acetone, ethanol, DCM, ether, chloroform, toluene and hexane, were tested using the present method when preparing the GQDs, as shown in Fig. 4 a and b. The resulting GQD solutions were colorless and transparent under visible light, and showed bright blue fluorescence under UV excitation of 365nm. The rGO hydrogels were very stable in all of the tested organic solvents and did not show any disintegration, indicative of the sufficiently strong π–π stacking interactions between the large rGO sheets. The production yield of GQDs is depending on the polarity of organic solvents, ranging from ∼1.89% (Hexane) to ∼1.28% (Ethanol) (Fig. S6). Due to the low relative amount of hydrophilic polar groups in the GQDs, the lower the polarity of organic solvents, the higher the production yield of GQDs. The low-polarity organic solvents are also very convenient for preparing GQD polymer composite materials, such as the fluorescent polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) composite that was prepared as shown in Fig. 4c and d. The GQDs did not show any aggregation in the polymer system. Composite materials based on GQDs greatly extend the applications of GQDs to different fields.

Pure GQDs can be obtained by evaporating the organic solvent. Interestingly, at room temperature they formed a kind of deep yellow and highly viscous liquid that displayed the same blue photoluminescence under 365nm UV light as in solution (Fig. S7). As shown in Fig. 4e and f, a luminescent pattern can be prepared by drying a GQD solution on an etched quartz glass plate.

The photoluminescence spectra of GQDs in hexane are shown in Fig. 5 a. The GQDs displayed an excitation-dependent feature in our experiments, with the emission peak depending on the excitation wavelength. As the GQDs were excited at wavelengths from 275 to 385nm, the emission peak shifted from near-UV (340nm) to blue light (430nm), which is due to the optical selection of GQDs with different sizes and with surface defects [8]. The quantum yield of GQDs was 0.21, using 2-Amino-pyridine as standards (Supporting information, Table S1). The strongest emission appeared at 347nm (3.57eV) in the near-UV region when the GQDs were excited under 295nm UV radiation. To the best of our knowledge, except for Tang’s report on deep-UV-emission (4.10eV) GQDs [1], the GQDs in the current work display the shortest emission wavelength (Table S2).

The UV–visible absorption spectrum (Fig. 5b) of GQDs in hexane showed an absorption band at 261nm due to the π–π∗ transition of the aromatic CC bond, corresponding to a band gap of 4.75eV [5]. The GQDs in all the tested organic solvents yielded the strongest emission peak near 347nm, there are two obvious emission peaks (347nm and 383nm) for the GQDs in acetone and chloroform solvents. This exception may have resulted from acetone and chloroform being more polarity than the other solvents, hence having caused a slight aggregation of the GQDs and red shift of their emission. Previous reports have shown that the fluorescence of GQDs is mainly based on two mechanisms: defect state emission (surface energy traps, functional groups) and intrinsic state emission (electron-hole recombination, quantum size effect/zig-zag sites) [7,8,27–30]. Because only defect state emission highly depends on changes in the environment, such as solvent and pH [27], the negligible solvent-dependent behavior of the GQDs in the present work indicates that the intrinsic state emission is the dominant mechanism of the fluorescence. In addition, according to the theory reported by Sk et al [31], the presence of small sp2 clusters isolated within the sp3 carbon network may also contribute to the fluorescence of GQDs. Weng et al [32] also reported that the highly oxidized graphene also show 450–800nm fluorescence due to the similar mechanism of isolated sp2 clusters. The sp3 carbon network in Weng’s report is induced by direct oxidization of carbon atom in the basal plane. The size of the isolated sp2 clusters is 2–5nm. However, for the GQDs reported in this study, the sp3 carbon network is resulted from the hydrogenation of carbon atom in the basal plane, which is because that the over-oxidized ultrafine GO was reduced by H atom in the hydrothermal reduction process. The dimension of our GQDs is ∼2.5nm, thus we believed that the size of isolated sp2 clusters in one GQD molecule is much smaller than 2.5nm. Therefore, considering the different chemical environment and smaller size of isolated sp2 clusters, it is easy to understand the near-UV (347nm) emission property of our GQDs.

It is worth noting that the dried rGO hydrogel can also be used as a source of GQDs and showed the same fluorescence properties (Fig. S8), i.e., emitting at the same wavelength, as did the wet hydrogel. The volume of the dried rGO hydrogel was found to be only ∼1/43 of that of the wet rGO hydrogel, and this difference resulted from the collapse of the microporous structure in the transformation from the wet to the dried rGO hydrogel and from the related decrease in the surface ratio. This decrease of surface ratio likely explains the observed sharp decrease in the diffusion rate of GQDs in the dried rGO hydrogels, which were observed to take 2–3days to reach equilibrium, in contrast to the mere several hours for the wet rGO hydrogel. Storage of GQD samples in the dried hydrogel provides a great convenience by avoiding the instability of GQDs stored in the liquid phase.

We have developed a facile and “slow-release” method to prepare near-UV-emitting GQDs from graphene hydro-gels. Ultrafine GO fragments were prepared using long-duration oxidation and sonication and were then trans-formed into the GQDs by a hydrothermal reduction treatment. During this process, the GQDs adsorb on the surface of graphene hydrogel using π–π stacking interactions. Low-polarity organic solvents added to these graphene hydrogels caused the GQDs to separate from the surface of the hydrogel and dissolve the GQDs. These GQDs were determined to have dimensions of <4nm, a near-UV emission at 347nm, and few oxygen-containing functional groups, which indicates that they share a similar chemical structure with polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. This new method also greatly simplifies the post-processing of the general hydrothermal method, eliminating the need for centrifugation, filtration and dialysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Jingling Liu from SKKU SAINT for her kind help. This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technology (NRF-2013R1A1A2058893) and the Global Frontier R&D Program on Center for Multiscale Energy System (NRF-2012M3A6A7054863).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2015.06.033.
